title: Cisco ASA - Core Syslog Message Volume Drop
spl:
`cisco_asa`
    message_id IN (302013, 302014, 609002, 710005)
    | eval msg_desc=case(
      message_id="302013","Built inbound TCP connection",
      message_id="302014","Teardown TCP connection",
      message_id="609002","Teardown local-host management",
      message_id="710005","TCP request discarded"
    )
    | bin _time span=15m
    | stats count values(msg_desc) as message_description
                  values(dest) as dest
      by _time message_id
    | xyseries _time message_id count
    | `cisco_asa___core_syslog_message_volume_drop_filter`
description: This detection identifies Cisco ASA - Core Syslog Message Volume Drop. It supports security monitoring and incident response. Part of Splunk Security Essentials (ESCU) where applicable.
value: This detection identifies Cisco ASA - Core Syslog Message Volume Drop. It supports security monitoring and incident response. Part of Splunk Security Essentials (ESCU) where applicable.
grandmaExplanation: This detection identifies Cisco ASA - Core Syslog Message Volume Drop — so we know when protection is off, out of date, or bypassed.
implementation: ESCU Hunting detection — not designed for automated alerting. Run ad-hoc or on a low-frequency schedule from Splunk Enterprise Security for analyst-driven investigation. Requires Cisco ASA Logs ingested and CIM-normalized.
detailedImplementation:
Enterprise Security Detection Rule

"Cisco ASA - Core Syslog Message Volume Drop" is a Hunting detection designed for analyst-driven threat hunting rather than automated alerting, sourced from the Splunk Enterprise Security Content Update (ESCU). Hunting detections are not intended for automated alerting. They are run on-demand or on a low-frequency schedule by security analysts investigating specific threat hypotheses.

Prerequisites

• Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x or later with the ES Content Update (ESCU) app installed and up to date.
• Data sources: Cisco ASA Logs. Must be ingested into Splunk and normalized to the Common Information Model (CIM) via the appropriate Technology Add-on.
• MITRE ATT&CK: T1562. Review the ATT&CK matrix for adjacent techniques to identify detection coverage gaps in your environment.

Deployment

1. In Enterprise Security, navigate to Configure → Content → Content Management.
2. Search for "Cisco ASA - Core Syslog Message Volume Drop" or filter by Analytic Story.
3. Hunting detections are typically left disabled for automated scheduling. Instead, run them on-demand from the Search bar or schedule at low frequency (daily or weekly).
4. Review results manually — hunting detections cast a wider net and expect analyst judgment to separate signal from noise.
5. When results warrant further investigation, create a Notable Event manually or initiate your incident response workflow.

Tuning and False Positive Management

Known false positives for this detection: None explicitly documented by the upstream ESCU detection — review alerts in business context before suppressing, and consult the linked references for vendor guidance.

• Hunting detections are expected to produce broader result sets that require analyst interpretation. Focus on refining the search scope (time range, specific hosts or users) rather than eliminating all noise.
• Maintain a hunting journal documenting hypotheses tested, results found, and detection improvements identified.
• Share high-fidelity findings with the detection engineering team to convert recurring hunting patterns into automated TTP or Anomaly detections.

Analyst Response Workflow

When reviewing Hunting results:

1. Run the search manually or review scheduled results. Evaluate each returned event against your threat hypothesis — not every result indicates compromise.
2. Cross-reference findings with current threat intelligence, recent security advisories, and outputs from other detection sources.
3. If suspicious activity is confirmed, create a Notable Event or escalate directly to your incident response process with the supporting evidence.
4. Document the hunting exercise: hypothesis, data sources queried, time range, findings, and any detection engineering improvements identified.

Validation

Confirm the required data sources are flowing:

```spl
| tstats count where index=* by index, sourcetype | sort -count
```

Verify the detection is enabled and firing:

```spl
index=_audit action="alert_fired" ss_name="*"
| stats count by ss_name, trigger_time | sort -trigger_time
```
